title: Huawei's Scaling Law, EU's Trade Offensive, and Space-Based Missile Tracking
subhead: Huawei claims a new path to advanced chips without Western tools, Brussels signals a harder China trade line, and SpaceX lands a $4.16B contract for space-based missile tracking — marking a significant day in semiconductor innovation, transatlantic trade policy, and orbital defense infrastructure.

sections.heading: Huawei's Scaling Law Gamble
sections.body:
Huawei Technologies is betting on a new computational scaling law to achieve advanced chip performance without relying on cutting-edge Western manufacturing tools, according to a South China Morning Post analysis. The approach, detailed by SCMP's tech correspondent, represents Huawei's response to US export controls that have blocked access to advanced lithography equipment.

The company's LogicFolding architecture, unveiled earlier this week, aims to produce chips by 2031 that match the performance of 1.4-nanometre technology — but using older manufacturing processes that remain accessible to Chinese foundries. Peking University announced on Tuesday a prototype EDA tool compatible with LogicFolding, providing domestic software support for the architecture.

The strategy essentially trades manufacturing precision for computational complexity: using more transistors arranged in novel configurations to achieve comparable performance. Whether this approach can meaningfully substitute for advanced process nodes remains unproven, but it represents one of the most concrete alternative roadmaps yet articulated by a Chinese tech champion.
sections.whyItMatters: Huawei isn't waiting for US export controls to ease — it's building an alternative technical path. The question isn't whether this works perfectly, but whether it works well enough to keep Huawei competitive in critical markets.

sections.heading: EU Signals Harder China Trade Line
sections.body:
The European Commission agreed on Friday to a tougher approach to China trade relations during a rare Beijing-focused debate among Brussels' leadership, SCMP reports. Trade chief Maros Sefcovic presented the case for stronger defensive measures against what officials described as a new 'China shock' to European industries.

The Commission may develop a new instrument compelling companies in critical sectors to diversify both the number and locations of suppliers — essentially mandating supply chain resilience. Sefcovic is also expected to increase use of safeguard measures in sectors under severe pressure from Chinese overcapacity, particularly chemicals and machinery. Unlike anti-dumping investigations that require lengthy probes, safeguards can be deployed faster and are harder for dissenting member states to block.

Commission President Ursula von der Leyen reportedly supports the more robust approach. Beijing vowed retaliation, according to the report.
sections.whyItMatters: Brussels is moving from rhetoric to concrete trade defense tools. The use of safeguards — faster and harder to block than anti-dumping measures — signals the EU is preparing for systemic confrontation with Chinese overcapacity rather than case-by-case disputes.

sections.heading: SpaceX Wins $4.16B Space-Based Missile Tracking Contract
sections.body:
The US Space Force awarded SpaceX a $4.16 billion contract to develop and deploy a space-based sensor network for advanced missile threat indication, The War Zone reports. The contract covers the AMTI (Advanced Missile Threat Indicator) system, with early operational capability targeted for 2028.

The award represents one of the largest single contracts for space-based missile tracking and marks SpaceX's deepening role in national security space infrastructure beyond launch services. The system will provide persistent, global tracking of hypersonic and ballistic missile threats — capabilities that ground-based radars cannot match.

The contract comes as both Russia and China continue developing hypersonic weapons that travel at speeds and trajectories that challenge traditional missile defense architectures.
sections.whyItMatters: Space-based missile tracking is becoming a core pillar of US missile defense architecture. SpaceX is no longer just a launch provider — it's now a prime contractor for critical defense sensor networks.

sections.heading: Alibaba's Voice AI Cracks Global Top 5
sections.body:
Alibaba's Fun-Realtime-TTS-Preview voice model has reached fifth place on Artificial Analysis's Speech Arena leaderboard with a score of 1,190, according to SCMP. It's the only Chinese-engineered voice system in the global top five, beating out OpenAI and xAI in blind user evaluations.

The model supports more than 30 languages, seven major Chinese dialects, and over 20 regional accents — a particular strength given the complexity of Chinese linguistic diversity. Separately, Alibaba's Fun-Realtime-ASR model ranked first on the Word Error Rate index with just 1.8 percent transcription errors (fewer than two words per hundred transcribed incorrectly).

The Speech Arena benchmark is operated by Artificial Analysis, a San Francisco-based AI evaluation organization backed by former GitHub CEO Nat Friedman and Google Brain founder Andrew Ng.
sections.whyItMatters: Chinese AI firms are demonstrating competitive parity in specialized domains. Voice AI may not grab headlines like large language models, but it's critical for consumer applications — and Alibaba is showing it can match or exceed Western performance in areas where Chinese linguistic complexity gives it natural advantages.

sections.heading: Hong Kong Exchange Rides Chip Boom at 40
sections.body:
The Hong Kong Stock Exchange marked its 40th anniversary on Thursday, with HKEX Chairman Carlson Tong noting the exchange has evolved from 'a locally focused exchange into a global superconnector,' SCMP reports. The exchange now oversees $6.1 trillion in market capitalization.

The 'memory chip supercycle' — driven by AI compute demand — has fueled a surge of Chinese tech IPOs in Hong Kong. Brokers see the semiconductor boom offsetting intensifying competition for listings from US and mainland markets.

Cathay Pacific, which was the first company to list on the unified exchange in May 1986, participated in the anniversary ceremony — a reminder of how Hong Kong's financial infrastructure has grown alongside its corporate champions.
sections.whyItMatters: The AI boom is creating winners beyond the chip designers themselves. Hong Kong's exchange is capturing semiconductor IPO demand as Chinese tech firms seek capital outside US markets — a financial infrastructure story that parallels the semiconductor sovereignty competition.

sections.heading: Alibaba Launches MuleRun AI Agent Platform
sections.body:
Alibaba unveiled MuleRun, a new AI agent platform designed to offer 'safer adoption' of agentic AI for enterprise users, SCMP reports. The platform is positioned as a response to privacy and security concerns that emerged with earlier AI agent tools like OpenClaw earlier this year.

MuleRun offers a one-stop service for users to access multiple AI agents for research, reports, coding, and other tasks. The platform serves businesses and users in 43 countries including China, Brazil, and Mexico. It was developed by a team led by Chen Yusen, an Alibaba Cloud vice-president who previously oversaw the company's global expansion.

Initially launched last September as an 'AI agent marketplace,' MuleRun has transitioned to a broader productivity platform offering agents for video generation, stock analysis, and social media management.
sections.whyItMatters: Alibaba is trying to build the 'app store for AI agents' with enterprise safety baked in. If MuleRun succeeds, it positions Alibaba as a platform provider in the agentic AI space — potentially more defensible than competing on raw model performance alone.

bottomLine: Today marked concrete moves on multiple tech-power fronts: Huawei articulated a technical path around US export controls, Brussels signaled it would use faster and harder trade defense tools against Chinese overcapacity, and SpaceX secured a $4.16B contract for space-based missile tracking. Alibaba's voice AI and agent platform showed Chinese firms competing effectively in specialized domains. The semiconductor supercycle continues to lift Hong Kong's exchange. None of these stories represent sudden breakthroughs, but each advances ongoing structural competition in semiconductors, trade policy, orbital infrastructure, and AI capabilities.
sources: South China Morning Post, The War Zone, Artificial Analysis
